specification.topic-statement: The multiline cursor navigation system moves the cursor vertically between lines and to line boundaries within the input buffer's text.

scope.boundary: This topic reads and modifies the text content and byte length of the input buffer.
This topic updates the cursor's byte offset and screen position by delegating to the cursor positioning system. It reads the current byte offset from the cursor.
After text-modifying operations, this topic signals that the layout cache is invalid.
This topic deletes individual bytes from the input buffer's backing byte array by index.

field.name: cursor byte offset
field.note: current position in text, in bytes

field.name: target column
field.note: saved grapheme column for sticky vertical movement; 0 means not set or column 0

field.name: physical lines
field.note: cached layout value; modified by layout invalidation

behavior.grapheme-unit-counting:
Several operations within this topic count grapheme units within a byte range to determine column positions.
Each UTF-8 byte sequence — whether ASCII (1 byte), two-byte, three-byte, or four-byte — is counted as exactly one grapheme unit.
Multi-codepoint grapheme clusters (such as emoji with modifiers or combining characters) are counted as multiple grapheme units, one per codepoint. The system does not join codepoints into clusters.
ANSI escape sequences are not detected or excluded during grapheme counting. If ANSI sequences are present in the text, the column positions calculated by vertical navigation operations will not correspond to visual columns. (The layout system's display-width calculation does exclude ANSI sequences, but this topic's grapheme counting does not.)
If an invalid UTF-8 byte is encountered (a byte that does not match the leading byte pattern of any valid UTF-8 sequence), the process terminates immediately with a diagnostic message.
behavior.cursor-up:
The cursor moves to the previous line at the same grapheme column, if possible.
If the input buffer is empty, the operation is a no-op. If the cursor is on the first line (no newline exists before the cursor position), the operation is a no-op.
The current column position is counted as the number of grapheme units from the start of the current line to the cursor. If the target column has not yet been saved (the stored value is zero), it is set to the current column.
The stored target column uses zero for both "not yet saved" and "column zero." When the cursor is at the first grapheme position of a line, the saved value is zero, which is indistinguishable from the initial unset state. On subsequent vertical moves, the system behaves as if the target column has not been saved, which causes it to use the current column each time. The observable effect is correct — the cursor remains at column zero — but the target-column memory does not persist across moves when the cursor is at the start of a line.
The previous line is bounded by its own start (position after the newline preceding it, or position zero) and the newline character that terminates it (the byte immediately before the start of the current line). The length of the previous line in grapheme units is counted.
The desired column is the saved target column if it is greater than zero, otherwise the current column. If the previous line is shorter than the desired column, the cursor is placed at the end of the previous line. Otherwise, it is placed at the desired column.
The cursor byte offset and cursor screen position are updated to the new position.
behavior.cursor-down:
The cursor moves to the next line at the same grapheme column, if possible.
If the input buffer is empty, the operation is a no-op. If the cursor is on the last line (no newline exists at or after the cursor's current line end), the operation is a no-op.
Column saving follows the same rules as cursor-up, including the column-zero ambiguity.
The next line begins at the byte immediately after the newline that ends the current line. The end of the next line is the position of its terminating newline, or the end of text if there is none. The length of the next line in grapheme units is counted.
Column clamping follows the same rules as cursor-up.
The cursor byte offset and cursor screen position are updated.
behavior.cursor-to-line-start:
The cursor moves to the first byte of the current line.
If the input buffer is empty, the operation is a no-op. If the cursor is already at the line start, the operation is a no-op.
Otherwise, the cursor is moved to the position immediately after the most recent newline before the cursor (or to position zero if no such newline exists). The target column is reset to zero.
behavior.cursor-to-line-end:
The cursor moves to the position of the newline that terminates the current line, or to the end of the text if the current line is the last line.
If the input buffer is empty, the operation is a no-op. If the cursor is already at the line end, the operation is a no-op.
Otherwise, the cursor is moved to the line end position. The target column is reset to zero.
behavior.kill-to-line-end:
All text from the cursor position to the end of the current line is deleted, not including the newline character itself.
If the input buffer is empty, the operation is a no-op. If the cursor is already at or past the line end position, the operation is a no-op.
Otherwise, each byte from the cursor position up to (but not including) the newline character is deleted from the byte array one at a time, starting at the cursor position on each iteration. After deletion, the cursor is synchronized to the (now-modified) text at the same byte offset. The target column is reset to zero. The layout cache is invalidated.
behavior.kill-line:
The entire current line is deleted, including its terminating newline if present.
If the input buffer is empty, the operation is a no-op.
Otherwise, the current line's start and end positions are found. If a newline follows the line end, it is included in the deletion range. Each byte in the range (from line start to the byte after the newline, or to line end if no newline follows) is deleted from the byte array one at a time, starting at the line start position on each iteration.
After deletion, the cursor is placed at the position where the deleted line began (which is now the beginning of what was the following line, or the end of the buffer if the deleted line was the last). If this position exceeds the new text length, the cursor is clamped to the text length. The target column is reset to zero. The layout cache is invalidated.
